svg.text: pgstatindex (PostgreSQL 18, btree): bloat and fragmentation on disk
Btree leaf page anatomy (one block, BLCKSZ = 8192 bytes)
max_avail = pd_special − SizeOfPageHeaderData (≈ BLCKSZ − 40, constant per leaf)
free_space = pd_upper − pd_lower (PageGetExactFreeSpace) — varies per leaf
How pgstatindex_impl turns leaves into numbers
low density ⇒ leaves mostly empty ⇒ bloated
fragment = leaf with btpo_next != P_NONE AND btpo_next < blkno
(right sibling lives on an earlier disk block)
deleted_pages = BTP_DELETED set (any leaf or internal)
Three indexes side by side: same key set, same 6 disk blocks, different states
Each index has 1 metapage (M, blue) + 1 internal root (R, yellow) + 4 leaves (L, green fill = data, light gray = free space). Arrows below each index trace the leaf chain via btpo_next.

g.text: leaves nearly full • leaf chain in disk order
A → B → C → D, every next > current
leaves are nearly full → low bloat
Both metrics in a healthy range.
leaves mostly empty • leaf chain still in order
heavy DELETEs / UPDATEs without REINDEX or vacuum recovery
chain ordering is fine — bloat is about
most of each leaf is unused → bloat
same as healthy — bloat does not
add blocks here, just wastes the ones we have
density << 90% on a freshly built index
leaves nearly full • leaf chain crosses backward
page splits inserted in non-key order over time
logical key order: A → B → C → D
density alone does not flag this
range scans on this index do extra
disk seeks to follow the leaf chain.
